perturbation.original_text: 8. Applicable Law - Arbitration This Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accor dance with the laws of the People ’s Republic of China without regard to principles of conflict of law, and any dispute arising out of this Agreement or In connection with this Agreement shall be referred to Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre (HKIAC) for arbitration in accordance with Hong Kong Interna tional Arbitration Centre (HKIAC) administered arbitration rules in force at the time of application. The arbitrators shall be one (1). The place of arbitration shall be in Shen Zhen, China . The language used in arbitration proceeding shall be English Lang uage. Both parties will keep in strict confidential of all information relating to the dispute. Both parties shall continue to perform their respective obligations which are not in dispute. Nothing here shall be interpreted as preventing the aggrieved party from seeking court injunction, interim measures, and protective order from competent court in relevant jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Applicable Law - Arbitration This Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accor dance with the laws of the People ’s Republic of China without regard to principles of conflict of law, and any dispute arising out of this Agreement or In connection with this Agreement shall be referred to Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre (HKIAC) for arbitration in accordance with Hong Kong Interna tional Arbitration Centre (HKIAC) administered arbitration rules in force at the time of application. The arbitrators shall be one (1). The place of arbitration shall be in Shen Zhen, China . The language used in arbitration proceeding shall be English Lang uage. Both parties will keep in strict confidential of all information relating to the dispute. Both parties shall continue to perform their respective obligations which are not in dispute.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'Nothing here shall be interpreted as preventing the aggrieved party from seeking court injunction, interim measures, and protective order from competent court in relevant jurisdiction,' the contract now restricts the parties' ability to seek urgent remedies from a court, potentially violating laws that guarantee access to courts for emergency relief.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 65 outlines the procedures for seeking preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders in federal court. By omitting the clause allowing parties to seek such relief, the contract attempts to limit access to these crucial legal remedies, which is in contradiction with the established court rules which allow parties to seek such urgent measures.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit allowance for parties to seek court injunctions, interim measures, and protective orders, which contradicts the principle that parties should have access to courts for urgent relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing FRCP Rule 65, outlines the procedures for seeking preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders. By omitting the clause allowing parties to seek such relief, the contract attempts to limit access to these remedies. This creates a legal contradiction because the original contract acknowledges the right to seek such remedies, while the modified version removes this allowance, potentially hindering a party's ability to protect its interests through court intervention. The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts, aiming for a "just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Removing the clause restricts access to these procedures, directly contradicting the aim of just and speedy resolution.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Remedies for Breach of Confidentiality and Indemnification , Liquidated damages In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party shall pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage shall not exceed 1,0000,000 USD. The application of liquidated damage shall not prejudice the aggrieved party ’s right to claim for all the losses and shall not prejudice all the liabilities and obligations incurred . Nothing he re shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities .
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Remedies for Breach of Confidentiality and Indemnification , Liquidated damages In the event one of the Parties or its officers, agents, employees or any of its subsidiaries or their officers, agent s or employees violates the restrictions against disclosure or use of the Confidential Information defined herein, Recipient or such entity or individual(s) as the case may be, shall and do hereby consent to the entry of an injunction by a court of compete nt jurisdiction against any further disclosure or use of the Confidential Information, and shall pay the The Recipient, including without limitation to any of its employees, subsidiaries , agents, subcontractors, officers shall indemnify and hold harmless against the Discloser against any unpermitted disclosing and publicizing. In event of any disclosing or publicizing the confidential information or breaching any obligations under this Agreement, the breaching party shall mitigate the losses and take for a ll reasonable measure, including the court injunction to prevent the disclosure and spread of confidential information upon the request of aggrieved party. The breaching party shall pay to non -beaching party a liquidated damage of 10,000 USD per occurrence . The total liquidated damage shall not exceed 1,0000,000 USD.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'Nothing here shall be interpreted as excluding consequential losses the aggrieved party may suffer, including without limitation to loss of profit, loss of goodwill, loss of business opportunities,' this clause attempts to limit the types of damages recoverable, which may contradict general principles of contract law that allow for the recovery of foreseeable consequential damages. This is especially problematic if the exclusion of consequential damages is deemed unconscionable under applicable law.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-719(3) states that consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. By completely removing the clause that clarifies that consequential damages *are not* excluded, the contract becomes more ambiguous and potentially leads to the total limitation of remedies available, increasing the risk of a court finding the limitation unconscionable and unenforceable. This is especially relevant in commercial contexts where consequential damages (like lost profits) can be significant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit statement that consequential damages are not excluded, which increases the risk that a court might find a limitation of remedies unconscionable and unenforceable. UCC § 2-719(3) states that "Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable." By removing the original clause, the agreement opens itself to a possible interpretation that consequential damages *are* excluded, thus creating a higher chance of unconscionability. This contradicts the principle of allowing for consequential damages unless explicitly and consciously waived, creating a legal contradiction by omission. The scraped snippet supports that consequential damages are subject to a limitation on exclusion, which is not explicitly included. By omitting that consequential damages are not excluded, the potential exclusion is implied.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Return of Documents and Materials The Parties hereby agree that all materials, including financial reports and analyses, customer list(s) programs, software, documents, reports, statistical data, trade secrets or similar printed, coded, electronic or magnetic material made by one of the Parties or to come into one of the Parties’ possession in the course of negotiations shall be returned to the other Party, upon conclusion of the negotiations.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Return of Documents and Materials The Parties hereby agree that all materials, including financial reports and analyses, customer list(s) programs, software, documents, reports, statistical data, trade secrets or similar printed, coded, electronic or magnetic material made by one of the Parties or to come into one of the Parties’ possession in the course of negotiations *may* be returned to the other Party, upon conclusion of the negotiations.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may' in the section regarding the return of documents, the contract creates ambiguity and weakens the obligation to return confidential materials at the end of the negotiation or relationship. This directly undermines the purpose of the NDA and increases the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure of confidential information. This is a problem where the contract is silent on how or when the receiving party has the obligation to return confidential material.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the contract states that it follows PRC law, many trade secrets disputes are handled in other jurisdictions, like California, where the company may have assets. California Civil Code § 3426.1 allows for injunctive relief against the misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the requirement to return confidential materials, the contract increases the risk of misappropriation. While the NDA still exists, the diminished obligation complicates enforcing the duty of confidentiality and obtaining injunctive relief because it introduces uncertainty about the expected behavior of the parties. Making the return optional opens the door to the argument that retention isn't necessarily misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' weakens the obligation to return confidential materials, increasing the risk of misappropriation. California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' as including the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, or the acquisition of a trade secret by improper means. By making the return of materials optional, the contract undermines the duty to maintain secrecy, as the retention of materials could be argued as not necessarily misappropriation, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise agreed in writing this Agreement shall gov ern Confidential Information disclosed by the Parties after the effective date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise agreed in writing this Agreement may gov ern Confidential Information disclosed by the Parties after the effective date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall' to 'may' in the section regarding the agreement governing confidential information disclosed after the effective date, the contract introduces ambiguity and weakens the assurance that the NDA will protect information disclosed after the agreement is signed. It implies that the NDA's protection for future disclosures is discretionary, potentially contradicting laws protecting trade secrets and confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836, part of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), creates a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. While the contract indicates PRC law governs, the DTSA could apply if misappropriation occurs within the U.S. or involves U.S. companies. By weakening the assurance that the NDA protects future disclosures, the contract reduces the predictability and enforceability of trade secret protection. This increased ambiguity makes it more difficult to prove that a trade secret was reasonably protected, which is a key element in a DTSA claim. The 'may govern' language opens the door for a defendant to argue that the information was not subject to reasonable measures to protect its secrecy, undermining the basis for a DTSA claim.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall' to 'may' weakens the mandatory nature of the agreement to govern confidential information, introducing ambiguity. While the provided law snippet primarily concerns the Economic Espionage Act, the Law Explanation argues that weakening the NDA's assurance makes it harder to prove reasonable protection of a trade secret. By making the governance of confidential information discretionary ('may govern'), the modified contract potentially undermines the ability to demonstrate reasonable measures were in place to protect trade secrets, a key element in a DTSA claim as described in the Law Explanation.
